query: Protests by the masses and students
reference_summary: Hundreds of students and other protesters gather at Sana'a University, calling for an end to the 32-year rule of President Ali Abdullah Saleh.The demonstrators were apparently inspired by the protests that led to the ouster of Tunisia's President.Yemeni protesters chant “the people want the regime to fall.”A day of antigovernment protests brings more than 20,000 people onto the streets in Sanaa.Tens of thousands of opposition activists demand the ouster of Saleh. Nearly 100,000 protest in Yemen's capital, deeming it the “Friday of No Return.”President Ali Abdullah Saleh's rule is further weakened when five key generals defect to join anti-government protesters.Thousands rally against him in “Day of Departure” protests.Thousands of anti-Saleh protesters march in the southern port of Aden. Huge crowds across Yemen demand Saleh leave.Opposition parades through Sanaa the bodies of 50 people it says were killed in clashes with Saleh's forces. Protesters have been struggling for months to overthrow President Ali Abdullah Saleh.Protests escalate as security forces fire on demonstrators, killing 21 and wounding dozens.

query: the military crackdown on the protests and the opposition
reference_summary: The government deploys military vehicles and extra troops in Sana'a, the capital, as police open fire on protesters, reportedly for the first time, killing at least one person and wounding 80 others.Protesters are reportedly fired upon by security forces disguised as pro-government supporters. As protest crowds swell, security forces fire on protesters in Sana'a, killing at least 52 people and wounding more than 100.Snipers kill 52 protesters among crowds that flock to a sit-in at Sanaa University after Friday prayers.After 45 pro-democracy protesters are killed in the deadliest day of civil unrest thus far, President Ali Abdullah Saleh declares Yemen to be in a state of emergency, granting the government the ability to detain civilians without charges and ban them from carrying weapons.Activists say police kill one anti-government demonstrator and wound scores more in Taez as Saleh calls for an end to the protests.Violence continues to escalate as police fire on unarmed protesters for the second consecutive day.Yemeni security forces shot dead at least 17 protesters as Gulf states offered to mediate and Washington appeared to be pulling the plug on Saleh. Security forces kill 13 protesters around Yemen.Loyalist forces fight Saleh opponents in Sanaa. Saleh's refusal to sign the power transfer deal sparks street battles in Sanaa between his security forces and a powerful tribal group, the Hashed tribal alliance, led by Sadeq al-Ahmar.At least 20 people are killed.Yemeni government forces push fighters opposed to President Ali Abdullah Saleh's regime from Sana's northern district. Loyalist forces open fire on protesters in the southern city of Taiz, killing at least 15.Saleh's forces disperse a sit-in in Taez, Yemen's second-largest city, killing at least 50 people.Protests escalate as security forces fire on demonstrators, killing 21 and wounding dozens. Security forces open fire on tens of thousands of demonstrators in Yemen's capital, Sana, killing at least 26 protesters in one of the bloodiest days of the 9-month-old rebellion against President Ali Abdullah Saleh.Security forces kill at least 20 people, some shot by snipers from rooftops, in a crowd of protesters demanding the removal of Saleh.At least 29 killed as police open fire on protesters and rival tribes clash in Sanaa.Four people killed in clashes across south Yemen, where separatists called for “civil disobedience” to protest against the presidential election.

query: The demands, negotiations and talks between the opposition and the government
reference_summary: Hundreds of students and other protesters gather at Sana'a University, calling for an end to the 32-year rule of President Ali Abdullah Saleh.Yemen's ruling party calls for dialogue with the opposition in a bid to stem antigovernment protests.Yemeni protesters chant “the people want the regime to fall.”Tens of thousands of opposition activists demand the ouster of Saleh.The opposition presents Saleh with a plan for a smooth transition of power, offering him a graceful exit.Saleh says he will draw up a new constitution to create a parliamentary system of government. An opposition spokesman rejects the proposal. Saleh promises a referendum on a new constitution to devolve power to parliament, but the opposition rejects the offer. Deputies backing President Saleh approve the state of emergency, but the vote is boycotted by the opposition.Saleh holds late-night talks with powerful army general Ali Mohsen al-Ahmar, who has defected to the opposition, but the two fail to agree.The opposition proposes a five-point plan for the army and security forces to be restructured by a vice president acting as temporary president.The opposition agrees to take part in a transitional government under a Gulf-negotiated peace plan for Saleh to step aside. Huge crowds across Yemen demand Saleh leave.Opposition tribal leaders say they are talking with the government and that a cease-fire is in effect, temporarily halting most of the fighting with security forces. President Saleh's forces and forces loyal to tribal leader Sheikh Sadiq al-Ahmar accept a Saudi-brokered cease-fire.Opposition calls for official transfer of power to VP. Despite their tenacity and desire to fashion a new order, protesters face a threat that it is the contest between President Ali Abdullah Saleh's family and a rival clan that will decide what change, if any, comes to Yemen.Opposition rejects government plan for dialogue, refusing to negotiate until Saleh signs transition deal.Government blames opposition tribal leader Hamid al-Ahmar for attack which wounded Saleh.A truce negotiated a week earlier quickly deteriorated.Yemeni protesters chant “the people want the regime to fall.”Protesters have been struggling for months to overthrow President Ali Abdullah Saleh.

query: Interference and response from other countries
reference_summary: World leaders criticize the crackdown. Qatari Prime Minister Sheikh Hamad bin Jassim al-Thani says the GCC will strike a deal for Saleh to leave.Russia and China reportedly prevent the council from publicly endorsing a draft statement calling on the parties in Yemen to “exercise restraint and enter a dialogue.” Yemeni security forces and anti-government protesters clash violently, as Persian Gulf and U.S. officials press for a deal that would allow longtime President Ali Abdullah Saleh to leave office with immunity.Saleh apologizes to the UAE but foreign governments pile criticism on him over his refusal to sign.Several countries, including United States, ask their diplomats to leave.G8 leaders at a summit in France call on Saleh to leave. International calls continue from several world powers calling for Mr. Saleh to leave office soon.Opposition calls for official transfer of power to VP, U.S. pushes for immediate democratic transition.U.S. counterterrorism official John Brennan meets Saleh in Riyadh, urges him to sign a transition plan.The U.S. Department of State approves Saleh's request to travel to the United States for medical treatment.

query: The intervention and response of the United Nations and the Gulf Cooperation Council
reference_summary: Qatari Prime Minister Sheikh Hamad bin Jassim al-Thani says the GCC will strike a deal for Saleh to leave.The U.N. Security Council meets on Yemen for the first time since the protests erupted in January. GCC mediators tell Yemen's opposition Saleh will only sign the deal as leader of his party. May 14 GCC secretary-general Abdullatif al-Zayani arrives in Sanaa on a three-day visit to revive the stalled deal.Yemen's main opposition coalition says the GCC plan to end the country's political crisis is “dead.”Five members of the ruling party sign the deal, but Gulf Arab states suspend it after Saleh asks for additional conditions and diplomats fail to persuade him to sign it.U.N. Security Council voices its “grave concern” about Yemen's deteriorating situation, urges restraint.The UN Security Council calls on Saleh to stand down.

Saleh promises reforms and says he will not seek a new mandate when his term ends in 2013.President Saleh fires five of 22 provincial governors, some for criticizing the crackdown on the protests.Saleh says he will draw up a new constitution to create a parliamentary system of government.Saleh had pledged not to seek reelection in 2013.Saleh says by the end of this year a new constitution will transfer power from the president to a parliamentary system.President Saleh declares a state of emergency.Saleh dismisses his government.As many ministers prepare to resign in protest over the recent deaths of unarmed demonstrators, President Ali Abdullah Saleh dismisses his cabinet in a move meant to signify governmental reform. Saleh warns against a coup, saying it could cause civil war.Saleh offers to step down by the end of 2011.He also proposes to hold a referendum on a new constitution, then a parliamentary election and presidential vote.Saleh holds late-night talks with powerful army general Ali Mohsen al-Ahmar, who has defected to the opposition, but the two fail to agree.In a speech to thousands of supporters, President Ali Abdullah Saleh says he may conditionally step aside and hand the nation to “safe hands” to avert further bloodshed after weeks of protests. Saleh holds talks with Mohammed al-Yadoumi, head of the Islamist Islah party, once a partner in his government.Saleh proposes staying in office until elections are held but transferring his powers to a caretaker. President Ali Abdullah Saleh hangs on even as protesters grow bolder and allies abandon him.Saleh agrees to step down in weeks in return for immunity from prosecution.Saleh appears to sabotage the peace plan by refusing to sign in his capacity as president. GCC mediators tell Yemen's opposition Saleh will only sign the deal as leader of his party. In a defiant speech to supporters in Sanaa, Saleh declares: “We will confront a challenge with a challenge” and denounces his opponents as “saboteurs.”Saleh apologizes to the UAE but foreign governments pile criticism on him over his refusal to sign. President Ali Abdullah Saleh refuses to sign the accord for him to step down with immunity, and a regional council says it is ceasing efforts for a deal. Saleh's refusal to sign the power transfer deal sparks street battles in Sanaa between his security forces and a powerful tribal group, the Hashed tribal alliance, led by Sadeq al-Ahmar.President Saleh's forces and forces loyal to tribal leader Sheikh Sadiq al-Ahmar accept a Saudi-brokered cease-fire.Saleh makes first televised appearance since attack, says he supports dialogue and welcomes power sharing. Saleh says he is committed to holding elections for a new president as soon as possible. Saleh empowers vice president to sign deal.Saleh says he will not step down as long as his key rivals remain in influential positions. Saleh signs a Gulf Cooperation Council-brokered agreement to step down, turning over power to his vice president.President Ali Abdullah Saleh says he will soon leave for the United States to help make way for elections for his replacement.

query: Rebel incidents within the government and the opposition
reference_summary: Seven legislators resign from Yemeni President Ali Abdullah Saleh's ruling General People's Congress party and say they will form an independent bloc. Nine legislators have left the party since protests began.As many ministers prepare to resign in protest over the recent deaths of unarmed demonstrators, President Ali Abdullah Saleh dismisses his cabinet in a move meant to signify governmental reform.Senior army commanders, including Saleh ally General Ali Mohsen, commander of the northwest military zone, say they have switched support to pro-democracy activists.President Ali Abdullah Saleh's rule is further weakened when five key generals defect to join anti-government protesters. Saleh holds talks with Mohammed al-Yadoumi, head of the Islamist Islah party, once a partner in his government.April 18 Ruling party members, including three former ministers, form Justice and Development Bloc to support the protests.Several opposition figures quit newly formed National Council, exposing division in anti-government movement.

query: The signing and negotiation of ceasefire agreements and transitional agreements
reference_summary: Influential clerics, tribal leaders and members of Yemen's opposition present a plan for a peaceful transition of presidential power.But some protesters demand that he step down immediately, and the opposition's proposal marks an attempt to find a middle ground. Saleh holds late-night talks with powerful army general Ali Mohsen al-Ahmar, who has defected to the opposition, but the two fail to agree.Qatari Prime Minister Sheikh Hamad bin Jassim al-Thani says the GCC will strike a deal for Saleh to leave.President Ali Abdullah Saleh agrees to an internationally negotiated plan to step down within 30 days in exchange for criminal immunity in the deadly crackdown on protests.Saleh appears to sabotage the peace plan by refusing to sign in his capacity as president.GCC mediators tell Yemen's opposition Saleh will only sign the deal as leader of his party.Five members of the ruling party sign the deal, but Gulf Arab states suspend it after Saleh asks for additional conditions and diplomats fail to persuade him to sign it. President Ali Abdullah Saleh refuses to sign the accord for him to step down with immunity, and a regional council says it is ceasing efforts for a deal. Saleh apologizes to the UAE but foreign governments pile criticism on him over his refusal to sign. Saleh's refusal to sign the power transfer deal sparks street battles in Sanaa between his security forces and a powerful tribal group, the Hashed tribal alliance, led by Sadeq al-Ahmar.Opposition tribal leaders say they are talking with the government and that a cease-fire is in effect, temporarily halting most of the fighting with security forces. Saleh's security forces and tribesmen agree to a truce after days of fighting kill at least 115. The deal includes a withdrawal of armed tribesmen from government buildings and moves to normalize life in Sanaa's Hasaba district.Yemen's capital and other cities erupt into violent chaos after a cease-fire collapses between forces loyal to President Ali Abdullah Saleh and tribal fighters.President Saleh's forces and forces loyal to tribal leader Sheikh Sadiq al-Ahmar accept a Saudi-brokered cease-fire. A truce negotiated a week earlier quickly deteriorated.U.S. counterterrorism official John Brennan meets Saleh in Riyadh, urges him to sign a transition plan.Opposition rejects government plan for dialogue, refusing to negotiate until Saleh signs transition deal.Ruling party approves changes to power transfer plan, which would transfer Saleh's powers to his vice president but give him three months to formally step down. It provides the basis for an interim government, which is to draft a new constitution and restructure the Saleh-controlled military.Saleh empowers vice president to sign deal.The opposition is sceptical of Saleh's call for further talks before the VP signs the deal. After months of unrest that have brought his country to the edge of civil war, Yemen President Ali Abdullah Saleh signs an agreement in Saudi Arabia to hand over power to his vice president in a deal that leaves him immune from prosecution in the deaths of protesters.
